title: March 2022 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 | Stats NZ
content:
## March 2022 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
03 June 2022, 12:00pm
March 2022 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 provides technical users with information about our approach to measuring the impact of COVID-19 in the forthcoming release of Gross domestic product: March 2022 quarter (scheduled for 16 June 2022).
This paper provides information about how we are responding to ongoing measurement challenges faced due to the nature of the economic shock caused by COVID-19. This paper follows similar updates about our methodology during COVID-19 that have been published in advance of the release of gross domestic product each quarter.
Information is presented in the following sections:
- Key events in the March 2022 quarter
- Data and methodology considerations
- Revisions to GDP(P) and GDP(E) components
Overview of sources and methods for quarterly gross domestic product: Updates and COVID-19 adjustments is an updated guide to the concepts and methods we are using to compile gross domestic product (GDP) during COVID-19 in New Zealand.

## Key events in the March 2022 quarter
The March 2022 quarter began with all of New Zealand in Orange traffic light setting except for Northland, which was in Red traffic light setting. On 23 January 2022, all of New Zealand moved to the red setting as Omicron cases spread within the community. The country remained at the red setting for the remainder of the March 2022 quarter.
The Red traffic light setting has more restrictions compared with the orange setting, such as capacity limits and distancing requirements at some venues. The use of the vaccination pass was required for venues over the whole quarter, as well as a mask mandate in indoor venues and public transport.
For all of the December 2021 quarter, Auckland was in higher alert level restrictions (alert level 3 and Red traffic light setting) while the rest of New Zealand was in lower alert level restrictions (alert level 2 and Orange traffic light setting). This excludes Northland and Waikato regions, which moved to different alert levels throughout the December 2021 quarter. Auckland, Northland, and Waikato had fewer restrictions on activity in the March 2022 quarter compared with the December 2021 quarter.
Easing of the border restrictions occurred in the later part of the March 2022 quarter with the removal of managed isolation and quarantine (MIQ) facilities and self-isolation requirements:
- From 3 March, vaccinated New Zealanders and eligible travellers entering the country no longer needed to self-isolate.
- From 4 March, New Zealanders and other eligible critical workers were able to enter the country.
- From 13 March, temporary visa holders including working holiday visa and recognised seasonal employers’ workers were able to enter the country without self-isolating.
These changes followed the December 2021 quarter, which had more restrictive border restrictions over the whole quarter, with no travel bubbles in place and isolation required in MIQ facilities for all who entered the country.

## Data and methodology considerations
As mentioned in previous papers, the abrupt economic shock caused by COVID-19 and measures to contain it posed challenges to our normal methods of measuring economic activity. The measurement of some GDP(P) and GDP(E) components continue to be impacted by the COVID-19 restrictions. We will review all components in the March 2022 quarter and, where appropriate, will use alternative data and methodologies in cases where conditions support it.
In previous quarters we have released the following information papers on our approach to the measurement challenges:
- December 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
- September 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
- June 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
- March 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
- December 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
- September 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
- June 2020 quarter Gross domestic product and COVID-19: Updated
- June 2020 quarter Gross domestic product and COVID-19
- COVID-19 lockdown and gross domestic product for March and June 2020 quarters
We have used alternative data sources in place of some of our usual data sources and methodologies since the March 2020 quarter, which was the first quarter that captured the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic in New Zealand. This was applied in the following cases:
- for components with methods that were unable to capture the sharp changes in activity that occurred due to COVID-19 prevention measures (for example, employment-based indicators, which were affected by the wage subsidy scheme)
- for components that experienced sharp changes in relationships between output and intermediate consumption
- for components that have a method of interpolation without a quarterly indicator.
As new data becomes available, we continue to evaluate the need for these adjustments. In some cases, we reverted components back to their usual quarterly method in the quarters that followed the June 2020 quarter COVID-19 lockdown. These decisions were based on the measured differences between adjusted and unadjusted series becoming small, as well as our judgement of the relative quality of data sources. The higher level COVID-19 alert level restrictions during the September 2021 quarter and subsequent quarters necessitated the return to alternative data sources for these components. As we produce the March 2022 release, we will be again reviewing which components in the current and previous quarters can revert to their usual quarterly method.
See Overview of sources and methods for quarterly gross domestic product: Updates and COVID-19 adjustments for a summary of the alternative indicators used. We will provide a further update on the approaches being used for GDP(P) and GDP(E) components on 10 June 2022, ahead of the release of Gross domestic product: March 2022 quarter.

## Revisions to GDP(P) and GDP(E) components

### Business price indexes revisions to the December 2021 quarter
As reported in Business price indexes: March 2022 quarter , an error in the processing of data for Business price indexes: December 2021 quarter was corrected during the compilation of the March 2022 quarter’s data. This will cause small revisions across various production components and to various constant price expenditure components in the upcoming Gross domestic product: March 2022 quarter release.

### Challenges with measuring low-level breakdowns of household consumption expenditure
Challenges with allocating travel credits and travel debits to household consumption expenditure (HCE) categories since the start of travel restrictions have resulted in increased uncertainty in the movements of HCE categories, especially in seasonally adjusted terms. This particularly affects travel-related categories such as restaurants and hotels, and recreation.
September 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 provides a full explanation of measuring low-level breakdowns of household consumption expenditure.
We will continue our use of additive outliers to treat unusual data points in seasonally adjusted series. We will test the March 2022 quarter series to determine where additive outliers need to be applied. Previous outliers applied to results for the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters and/or the March, June, September, and December 2021 quarters will be retained in the upcoming release Gross domestic product: March 2022 quarter .
Impact of COVID-19 on seasonally adjusted and trend series gives more information on additive outliers.

### Seasonal adjustment of travel services for the March 2022 quarter
As we have highlighted in previous quarters, the change from normal seasonal patterns is effectively captured in the seasonally adjusted aggregate GDP(E), meaning the full seasonal impact of reduced international tourism will be reflected in total GDP(E). However, the same impact will not be reflected in lower-level components, such as exports of travel services, because the multiplicative decomposition method used in seasonal adjustment means that smaller seasonal adjustments are made to components that have fallen in size.
The March quarter is typically the peak of the international tourist season; however, this usual seasonal pattern has been disrupted with the continued border restrictions due to COVID-19. This means we are expecting to see some impact on seasonally adjusted growth in the March 2022 quarter.
In GDP(P), travel-related activity is distributed across a range of industries and appears to have a softer seasonal contribution than what we observe in GDP(E).
Carol Mitchell 04 931 4600 info@stats.govt.nz
- Gross domestic product
- Overview of sources and methods for quarterly gross domestic product: Updates and COVID-19 adjustments 19 March 2026
